content:
# Genesis Chapter 46 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Translator:** SOPHIA (Consensus of EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27r–27v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_046_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_046_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 46–47

---

*Jacob Comes to Beersheba — The Last Patriarchal Vision (vv. 1–4)*

**46:1** And Israel set out with all that he had, and he came to Beersheba, and he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.

**46:2** And God spoke to Israel in visions of the night and said, "Jacob! Jacob!" And he said, "Here I am."

**46:3** And he said, "I am God, the God of your father. Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for I will make you into a great nation there.

**46:4** I myself will go down with you to Egypt, and I myself will surely bring you up again; and Joseph will put his hand on your eyes."

---

*The Departure from Canaan (vv. 5–7)*

**46:5** Then Jacob set out from Beersheba, and the sons of Israel carried Jacob their father, and their little ones, and their wives, in the wagons that Pharaoh had sent to carry him.

**46:6** And they took their livestock and their possessions, which they had gained in the land of Canaan, and they came to Egypt — Jacob and all his offspring with him:

**46:7** his sons, and his sons' sons with him, his daughters, and his sons' daughters — all his offspring he brought with him to Egypt.

---

*The Register of Those Who Went to Egypt — Seventy Souls (vv. 8–27)*

**46:8** Now these are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt — Jacob and his sons:

Reuben, Jacob's firstborn.

**46:9** And the sons of Reuben: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.

**46:10** And the sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman.

**46:11** And the sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.

**46:12** And the sons of Judah: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah — but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan. And the sons of Perez were Hezron and Hamul.

**46:13** And the sons of Issachar: Tola, Puvah, Jashub, and Shimron.

**46:14** And the sons of Zebulun: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.

**46:15** These are the sons of Leah, whom she bore to Jacob in Paddan-aram, together with his daughter Dinah; all the souls of his sons and his daughters: thirty-three.

**46:16** And the sons of Gad: Ziphion, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.

**46:17** And the sons of Asher: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, and Beriah, and Serah their sister. And the sons of Beriah: Heber and Malchiel.

**46:18** These are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah his daughter; and she bore these to Jacob: sixteen souls.

**46:19** The sons of Rachel, Jacob's wife: Joseph and Benjamin.

**46:20** And to Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, whom Asenath the daughter of Potiphera the priest of On bore to him.

**46:21** And the sons of Benjamin: Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.

**46:22** These are the sons of Rachel who were born to Jacob; all the souls: fourteen.

**46:23** And the sons of Dan: Hushim.

**46:24** And the sons of Naphtali: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.

**46:25** These are the sons of Bilhah, whom Laban gave to Rachel his daughter, and she bore these to Jacob; all the souls: seven.

**46:26** All the souls belonging to Jacob who came to Egypt, those who came out of his loins, besides the wives of Jacob's sons — all the souls were sixty-six.

**46:27** And the sons of Joseph who were born to him in Egypt were two souls. All the souls of the house of Jacob who came to Egypt: seventy.

---

*Arrival in Goshen — The Reunion (vv. 28–30)*

**46:28** And he sent Judah ahead of him to Joseph, to show the way before him to Goshen; and they came to the land of Goshen.

**46:29** Then Joseph harnessed his chariot and went up to meet Israel his father in Goshen. And he appeared to him and fell on his neck and wept on his neck a good while.

**46:30** And Israel said to Joseph, "Now let me die, since I have seen your face — that you are still alive."

---

*Joseph's Instructions about Goshen (vv. 31–34)*

**46:31** Then Joseph said to his brothers and to his father's household, "I will go up and tell Pharaoh, and I will say to him, 'My brothers and my father's household, who were in the land of Canaan, have come to me.

**46:32** And the men are shepherds of flocks, for they have been keepers of livestock, and they have brought their flocks and their herds and all that they have.'

**46:33** And when Pharaoh calls you and says, 'What is your occupation?'

**46:34** you shall say, 'Your servants have been keepers of livestock from our youth until now, both we and our fathers' — so that you may dwell in the land of Goshen, for every shepherd is an abomination to Egypt."

---

## SOPHIA Consensus Notes

### Rulings and Rationale

**1. "Joseph will put his hand on your eyes" — yashit yado al-einekha (v. 4): EMET upheld**

EMET renders the Hebrew literally: "Joseph will put his hand on your eyes." LOGOS renders "Joseph's hand shall close your eyes." PNEUMA renders "Joseph — his own hands will close your eyes." The Hebrew yashit yado al-einekha literally means "will put/place his hand upon your eyes" — the ancient death-closing gesture. EMET preserves both the singular "hand" (yado, not yadav/hands) and the concrete image of placing rather than "closing." LOGOS's "close" interprets the effect rather than rendering the action. PNEUMA follows the LXX's plural "hands" (χεῖρας) rather than the MT's singular yad — a departure from our MT-primary convention. The literal idiom is tender and clear; it does not need interpretation.

**2. "I myself will surely bring you up again" — anokhi + gam-aloh (v. 4): Blend**

The Hebrew ve'anokhi a'alkha gam-aloh contains two emphatic elements: the pronoun anokhi ("I myself") and the infinitive absolute aloh ("surely"). EMET captures "surely" but omits the second "I myself." LOGOS captures "I myself" but omits "surely." PNEUMA captures both anokhi and emphasis. Both elements are in the Hebrew; both should be in the English. SOPHIA renders: "I myself will surely bring you up again."

**3. "Souls" — nephesh in genealogical counting (vv. 15, 18, 22, 25, 26, 27): EMET upheld**

The Hebrew kol-nephesh ("all the souls") uses nephesh — the word for the whole animated self — as the counting unit throughout this register. "Seventy souls" is the canonical expression for Israel entering Egypt (Deut 10:22; Acts 7:14 uses psychas/souls). LOGOS and PNEUMA render nephesh as "persons," which is accurate but loses the Hebrew's way of counting people as whole living beings. The theological weight of the seventy-soul number is better served by preserving "souls."

**4. "Those who came out of his loins" — yotse'ei yerekho (v. 26): EMET upheld**

Hebrew yerekh means "thigh, loin" — a standard biblical euphemism for male generative origin (cf. Gen 24:2, 47:29). EMET preserves the idiom: "those who came out of his loins." LOGOS renders "those who came from his own body" — vague. PNEUMA renders "those who were his own flesh and blood" — a complete paraphrase that loses the specific anatomical reference. "Loins" preserves the Hebrew idiom and its physical concreteness.

**5. "To show the way before him" — lehorot lefanav (v. 28): LOGOS upheld**

The hiphil of yarah means "to direct, instruct, point out the way." EMET's "to direct" is accurate but slightly stiff. LOGOS's "to show the way" preserves the directional sense of the verb while being more natural in English. PNEUMA's "to prepare the way" introduces a different concept (preparation vs. direction/instruction). The Torah connection (yarah → Torah) is noted but does not require a different rendering.

**6. "A good while" — od (v. 29): LOGOS upheld**

The adverb od normally means "still, yet, again." Here it modifies weeping to indicate sustained duration. EMET's "again and again" implies discrete repeated bursts; the context describes one continuous, extended weeping. LOGOS's "a good while" follows the Tyndale-KJV tradition and captures the sustained quality of Joseph's embrace without over-specifying. PNEUMA's "a long time" is a simpler paraphrase. "A good while" balances faithfulness and English idiom.

**7. "Now let me die" — amutah ha-pa'am (v. 30): EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)**

The Hebrew amutah is a cohortative — a first-person volitional form expressing desire or willingness: "let me die." PNEUMA's "I can die" changes the grammatical mood from volitional to potential. Jacob is not merely stating ability but expressing the fulfillment of his deepest longing — death is now acceptable because reunion has occurred. The cohortative must be preserved: "let me die."

**8. "Jashub" — yov/yashub (v. 13): LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)**

EMET renders the MT ketiv יוֹב as "Job." LOGOS and PNEUMA follow the standard English convention "Jashub," which reflects the qere reading and harmonizes with the parallel passage in Num 26:24 and 1 Chr 7:1 (where the name appears as Jashub/Yashub). SOPHIA follows the majority: "Jashub."

**9. "Shepherds of flocks" — ro'ei tson (v. 32): EMET upheld**

EMET renders the full phrase: "shepherds of flocks" (ro'ei tson). LOGOS abbreviates to "shepherds." The tson ("flocks/sheep") specification matters because v. 34 specifies kol-ro'eh tson ("every shepherd of flocks"), and the LXX's πᾶς ποιμὴν προβάτων ("every shepherd of sheep") also retains it. Preserving "of flocks" in v. 32 sets up the connection to v. 34.

**10. Verse-by-verse agreements (3-0):**

- v. 1: "sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac" — all three preserve the specific invocation of Isaac's God at Isaac's altar-place (Beersheba).
- v. 2: "Jacob! Jacob!" / "Here I am" — all three preserve the doubled name and hinneni. The last patriarchal hinneni of Genesis.
- v. 3: "I am God, the God of your father" — all three render El ha-Elohei avikha consistently.
- vv. 8-27: The genealogy shows near-total agreement across all three translators, with names and counts matching precisely.
- v. 34: "every shepherd is an abomination to Egypt" — all three preserve to'evah as "abomination."

---

### LXX Variants Summary (Vaticanus, Pages 46–47)

| Verse | MT Reading | LXX (Vaticanus) | SOPHIA Decision |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 46:1 | "the God of his father Isaac" | "the God of his father Isaac" | Agreement; no variant |
| 46:3 | "I am God [ha-El], the God of your father" (singular) | "I am the God of your fathers" (plural, πατέρων) | Follow MT: singular avikha points specifically to Isaac — Jacob is at Isaac's altar |
| 46:4 | "I myself will go down... I myself will bring you up" (double anokhi) | "I will go down... I will bring you up" (emphatic force softened) | Follow MT: the double anokhi is the theological heart of the promise |
| 46:4 | "Joseph will put his hand [yado, singular] on your eyes" | "Joseph will place his hands [χεῖρας, plural] on your eyes" | Follow MT: singular "hand" is more intimate |
| 46:10 | Sons of Simeon: Ohad listed | LXX omits Ohad; adds Jachin as "Jarib" | Follow MT; Ohad absent in later census (Num 26) but present here |
| 46:13 | Sons of Issachar: Yov (יוֹב) | LXX: Iasub (Ἰασούβ) | Follow convention: "Jashub" (harmonizing with Num 26:24) |
| 46:16 | Sons of Gad: Ziphion (tsiphyon) | LXX: Saphon (Σαφών) | Follow MT transliteration |
| 46:20 | Joseph's sons: Manasseh and Ephraim only | LXX adds Machir, Gilead, Shuthelah, Taam, Edom (+5 persons) | Follow MT: LXX harmonizes forward from Num 26 and 1 Chr 7 |
| 46:21 | Sons of Benjamin: 10 names listed | LXX rearranges; moves Ard and Naaman to grandsons of Benjamin | Follow MT list |
| 46:22 | Rachel's line total: 14 | LXX: 18 (with additions) | Follow MT: 14 |
| 46:26 | "sixty-six souls" | "sixty-six souls" (ἑξήκοντα ἕξ) | Agreement on subtotal |
| 46:27 | "seventy" (שִׁבְעִים) | "seventy-five" (ἑβδομήκοντα πέντε) | Follow MT: 70 is the theologically resonant number (Table of Nations) |
| 46:28 | "to show the way before him to Goshen" (lehorot lefanav) | "to meet him at the city of Heroes, in the land of Rameses" | Follow MT: LXX replaces the Hebrew idiom with a geographic specification |
| 46:29 | "wept on his neck a good while" (od) | "wept much" (κλαύσας κλαυθμῷ) — cognate accusative | Follow MT: od as sustained duration; LXX's cognate accusative is a different construction |
| 46:34 | "every shepherd is an abomination to Egypt" (to'evat Mitsrayim) | "every shepherd of sheep is an abomination to Egyptians" (βδέλυγμα) | Near-equivalent: LXX specifies "sheep" (probaton) and uses bdelygma for to'evah |

---

### The 70 vs. 75 Discrepancy — A Critical Note

The MT total of 70 (shiv'im nefesh) is theologically resonant: 70 is the number of the nations listed in the Table of Nations (Gen 10), signaling that Israel-as-seventy is the covenant counterpart to the world's seventy peoples. It is also the number of elders appointed by Moses (Num 11:16) and the symbolic number of completeness in Second Temple tradition.

The LXX total of 75 is reached by adding, in vv. 20 and 27, five Egyptian-born descendants of Joseph: Machir son of Manasseh, Gilead son of Machir, and Shuthelah, Taam, and Edom, sons of Ephraim. These names appear in later Israelite tradition (Num 26:28-37; 1 Chr 7:14-27) but as later genealogical entries. The LXX translator — or the Vorlage he used — appears to have harmonized forward, incorporating descendants known from later tribal lists into the Egyptian migration count.

Stephen's speech in Acts 7:14 cites "seventy-five persons" (hebdomēkonta pente psychas), following the LXX. This demonstrates the LXX's authority in the early church and shows Luke's source text. Neither count is "wrong" — they represent two legitimate traditions with different counting conventions.

---

— SOPHIA, Consensus Translator | Project AI Bible
